one_line_takeaway: A taxonomy-driven survey arguing that autonomous-driving RL reward functions are non-standardized and structurally flawed (naive summation, no context-awareness, no validation), proposing Rulebooks, Reward Machines, and a validation framework as future directions — an AI-safety/control-objective review, not a cybersecurity paper.
key_finding: Reward functions in autonomous-driving RL are inconsistently and often inadequately formulated because there are no industry standards defining Safety, Comfort, Progress, or Traffic-Rules objectives for reward design, leading to ad hoc, non-standardized formulations. The two dominant structural weaknesses are (a) naive aggregation — most surveyed works simply SUM reward terms (giving safety the same weight as progress) with no encoding of priority or conflict resolution, and (b) lack of context-awareness — reward functions are hand-tuned for a specific use case (mostly urban driving) and none of the reviewed papers modify the reward to adapt to driving context. A recurring concrete failure the paper highlights (citing Knox et al.) is that dense progress rewards can make an agent rationally choose to crash into a static obstacle rather than wait idle, because accumulated progress penalties can exceed the collision penalty. The paper argues TTC is a better risk proxy than headway/distance, and that no automatic reward-validation framework exists (only Knox et al.'s eight manual sanity checks, which are impractical to verify in complex domains).
core_contribution: A literature review/survey that surveys reward functions used in state-of-the-art reinforcement learning (RL) approaches for autonomous driving, decomposes the reward functions found in prior work into individual reward terms, and assigns those terms to a predefined taxonomy of objective categories: Safety, Progress (efficiency), Comfort, and Traffic-Rules conformance (plus a residual 'model-performance' category for exploration-encouraging terms that it explicitly excludes from analysis). For each category the paper catalogs the common mathematical formulations found across ~50 cited works, notes the absence of applicable industry standards, and discusses formulation strengths/limitations. It then analyzes cross-cutting structural limitations (how objectives are aggregated and the lack of context-awareness) and proposes three future-work directions: Rulebooks for objective aggregation, Reward Machines for encoding driving context, and an automatic reward-function validation framework. The paper is authored by Abouelazm, Michel, and Zoellner (FZI / Karlsruhe Institute of Technology), published at IEEE IV 2025.
threat_model: Not applicable. This is not a security paper. It concerns reward-function design (an AI-safety / control-objective specification problem) for autonomous-driving RL agents, not an adversary, attack surface, or defense. It does briefly mention that adversarial 'critical scenario generation' methods from cited work (refs [71]-[74]) could seed a future reward-validation framework, but this is a cited direction, not the paper's own threat analysis, and no attacker/defender model is developed.
limitations: As a survey the paper contributes no empirical validation: the proposed remedies (Rulebooks, Reward Machines, a validation framework) are conceptual proposals, not implemented or evaluated, so their effectiveness for autonomous-driving reward design is asserted rather than demonstrated. The 'recommended' safety-reward formulation (sparse collision penalty + dense TTC/headway term) is described but not tested. The survey scope is bounded to reward terms mapping to its four categories and explicitly discards exploration/model-performance terms, and it acknowledges economic aspects (fuel efficiency, cost) are largely overlooked by the literature it reviews. The PDF (arXiv v3) also contains a broken citation placeholder ('[?]') and a duplicated paragraph recommending the safety formulation, indicating light editing polish. No quantitative comparison across the surveyed reward functions is offered.

quantitative_results: 94% of traffic accidents are attributed to human error (cited motivation from refs [1]-[3], not an original result)
Reward taxonomy uses 4 analyzed objective categories: Safety, Progress, Comfort, Traffic-Rules conformance (plus an excluded model-performance/exploration category)
One cited lexicographic deep-Q-network approach (Li & Czarnecki, [32]) prioritizes 4 goals: correct lane changes, safety, traffic-rule compliance, comfort
Cited progress reward (Paleja et al., [50]) additionally rewards reaching 40% and 60% of total progress
Knox et al.'s cited manual validation approach ([23]) consists of 8 sanity checks
